title: Growing calendula
subtitle: An easy hardy annual that self-seeds, blooms June to October, and earns the pot-marigold name.
excerpt: Calendula (Calendula officinalis, the pot marigold) is one of the easiest annuals to grow from seed. Sown direct in March or April it flowers from June and keeps flowering through to the first frost. The petals deadhead into next year's seed and the plant self-sows for next year.
sourceNotes:
Cultural notes drawn from two references:

• Royal Horticultural Society, calendula growing guide (rhs.org.uk/plants/calendula) for the planting window and the self-seeding habit.
• Mrs Earle, Pot-Pourri from a Surrey Garden (1897), §The cottage flower border. Project Gutenberg #14443. Calendula sits in the traditional English cottage garden alongside marigolds, nasturtiums, and other easy hardy annuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: A plant that completes its full life cycle in one growing year (germinates, flowers, sets seed, dies) and tolerates light frost. Hardy annuals can be sown direct outdoors as early as the ground is workable in spring.
Snipping off spent flowers before they set seed. The plant puts its energy into making more flowers instead of ripening seed, extending the flowering season by weeks.
A plant that drops viable seed at the end of its season, producing volunteer seedlings the following year without any sowing on the gardener's part.

content.text: Calendula (Calendula officinalis), traditionally called the pot marigold in British gardens, is one of the easiest annuals a beginner gardener can grow. The seed is large and easy to handle, it germinates in any decent soil within a fortnight, and once flowering starts in June it keeps flowering through to the first proper frost in October or November. The orange and yellow daisy-shaped flowers attract bees and hoverflies; the petals are edible and historically used as a saffron substitute. The whole plant is a friendly entry to growing things from seed.
What earns calendula its modern reputation is its
self-seeding

habit: leave a few flowers to ripen at the end of the season and the plant scatters its own seed for next year. A first-year sowing can become a permanent presence in a sunny border with no further work.
What you need

A packet of calendula seed (any variety; "Indian Prince" gives a deep orange with a darker eye, "Snow Princess" is creamy yellow, the standard mixed packet covers both ends). A patch of sunny ground or any container at least 25 cm wide and 25 cm deep. A trowel. A watering can. That's the entire kit.
Sowing in spring
Calendula is a
hardy annual

, so it can go in direct from March in southern England, April further north, and May for very cold gardens or anywhere the ground stays frozen later. There's no need for windowsill propagation; the seed germinates faster outdoors once spring soil temperatures are around 10 °C than it does on an indoor windowsill.
Rake the soil over the sowing area so the top inch is loose and crumbly. Calendula doesn't need rich soil; ordinary garden soil works.
Scatter the seed thinly on the surface or sow in shallow drills 1 cm deep. Cover lightly with soil and firm the surface with the back of a rake or the flat of your hand.
Water in if the soil is dry. Germination takes 10 to 14 days.
Thin the seedlings to about 30 cm apart once they have three or four true leaves. Closer planting gives shorter, leggy plants; wider spacing gives sturdier bushy ones.
Autumn sowing for an earlier crop

An autumn sowing in September overwinters as small seedlings and produces flowers two or three weeks earlier the following spring than a March sowing. The plants sit dormant through winter and put on growth as soon as light returns in February. This is the traditional cottage-garden trick for earliest cut flowers.
Growing through summer

Calendula is unfussy. Water in dry spells (especially in containers, which dry out faster than open ground) but don't feed: rich soil produces lots of leaves and few flowers. The main maintenance task is
deadheading

Snip off faded flowers every few days during the main flowering weeks. Each spent head removed prompts the plant to make another flower instead of ripening seed. A regularly deadheaded calendula will flower from late May until the first hard frost in October or November, producing dozens of blooms across the season.
Saving seed and self-seeding

Stop deadheading in September. The plants set crescent-shaped seedheads that ripen brown by October. Either snip a few heads, dry them indoors on a tray for a week, and store the dried seed in a labelled envelope for next year's sowing, or leave the heads to drop their seed where they're growing. Self-sown seedlings appear the following spring around the parent plant's footprint, often in greater numbers than you'd want.
Calendula seed remains viable for about 4 years in a cool, dry envelope; a small saved packet keeps the patch going indefinitely without buying fresh seed.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong on a first calendula sowing
attrs.intro: Three habits that turn a reliable plant into a disappointment.

items.symptom: Seedlings come up tall and leggy, then flop over
items.cause: Sowing too thickly; the seedlings competed for light from the start.
items.fix: Thin to one plant every 30 cm at the four-leaf stage. The remaining plants put on lateral growth and sturdy up within a fortnight. Don't waste effort trying to transplant the thinnings; calendula doesn't move well.

items.symptom: Plenty of foliage but very few flowers
items.cause: Soil is too rich, usually from heavy compost or manure dug in before sowing.
items.fix: Don't feed and don't enrich the soil. Calendula prefers poor to medium ground. If the patch is already too rich, simply wait. By midsummer the plant will start flowering despite the lush leaves.

items.cause: Powdery mildew, usually after a dry spell with crowded foliage.
items.fix: Cut back affected stems hard (to about 15 cm) and water deeply at the roots. The plant regrows and flowers again within three weeks. Powdery mildew on calendula is cosmetic, not fatal.

content.text: Where this goes next

The flowers are edible: pull the petals off the green base and scatter into salads, rice dishes, or bread doughs for a saffron-like colour with a mild bitter flavour. Calendula petals are also the canonical ingredient for an herbal infused oil for skincare and a folk-medicine balm. The next companion plants to grow are nasturtium (similarly easy, edible, self-seeding) and pot marigold's relative the African marigold (Tagetes) for the vegetable garden's pest-deterrent corner.
